message.content:
The shop smelled of cedar shavings, brass polish, and the faint, metallic tang of old oil. Dust motes danced in the single shaft of afternoon sunlight that pierced the gloom, illuminating hundreds of ticking faces.

Seven-year-old Leo stood on a stool, his chin resting on the glass counter. Before him sat Elias, a man whose fingers were as gnarled as the roots of an oak tree, yet moved with the precision of a spider weaving silk. He was adjusting the escapement of a grandfather clock that had stood silent for a decade.

"Mr. Elias?" Leo whispered, afraid to disturb the symphony of ticks.

Elias didn’t look up. "Yes, Leo?"

"Why do they all tick at different speeds? The big one goes *tock... tock...* but the little silver one goes *tick-tick-tick-tick*."

Elias finally looked up, his eyes magnified behind thick spectacles. He smiled, a slow cracking of dry earth. "They don’t tick at different speeds, Leo. They tick at the speed they were built to measure. The big clock measures hours. The small one measures seconds. But they are both measuring the same thing."

Leo frowned, tilting his head. "But time isn’t a thing. It’s just... now. And then it’s later."

Elias set down his tweezers. He picked up a small, intricate gear from a velvet tray. It was no bigger than a fingernail. "Hold this."

Leo took the gear. It was cold and heavy for its size.

"Time is not a river, Leo," Elias said softly. "People like to say that. That it flows. But look at this gear. Does it flow?"

"No," Leo said. "It sits there."

"Exactly. Time is not something
